exemplar.title: The effect of meditation on the reaction time (measured in ms) of boys and girls at the age of 16-18

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title: The effect of meditation on the reaction time (measured in ms) of boys and girls at the age of 16-18

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: The opening anecdote on sports reaction time demonstrates genuine curiosity and some personal connection to the topic.
The student clearly links the investigation to their own sporting experience, showing initial motivation.
weaknesses: The personal narrative is not revisited or expanded upon later in the report.
There is minimal independent shaping of methodology or data presentation beyond using existing online tools.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows limited personal engagement: a brief anecdote and a generic justification tied to sports interest, but no sustained reflection or initiative in design, implementation, or presentation. This aligns with strand assessments of Band 1 (limited evidence of engagement and significance).

strengths: The research question is relevant and identifies key variables (meditation and reaction time by gender).
A clear variable table distinguishes independent, dependent, and controlled factors.
weaknesses: Background research is superficial, lacking physiological or literature depth to support the hypothesis.
The methodology omits randomisation, sample-size justification, and statistical planning.
Safety and ethical considerations are mentioned only in passing via PAR-Q, without addressing consent or data privacy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the question is relevant, the report falls short in background depth, methodological rigor and ethical awareness. The strand scores (3 for focus, but 1 for background, methodology, safety) support placing exploration in the lower band. A score of 2 reflects minimal achievement beyond stating a focused question and listing variables, but significant gaps remain.

strengths: Charting by gender aids visual comparison of post-meditation effects.
weaknesses: No raw data tables are provided, preventing independent verification of results.
Data processing is limited to simple means without measures of spread or inferential statistics.
There is zero consideration of measurement uncertainty or instrument precision.
Interpretations are descriptive and sometimes contradictory, lacking statistical support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All analysis strands score at the lowest or near-lowest levels (raw data, processing, uncertainty, interpretation), consistent with a Band 1 overall. The work shows only minimal data handling and interpretation capabilities, warranting a score of 1.

strengths: The student provides realistic and relevant suggestions (increasing sample size, counterbalancing, controlling fatigue).
Critical headings signal reflection and add clarity to the evaluation section.
weaknesses: The conclusion is unsupported by quantitative evidence and lacks discussion of uncertainty.
Comparisons to existing studies remain superficial without tying numerical outcomes to literature.
Limitations are noted only at a basic level, missing methodological depth (e.g., latency, practice effects).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation contains a weak, unsupported conclusion (Band 1), superficial literature comparison (Band 1), and basic limitation discussion (Band 1), but realistic improvement suggestions lift it into the lower end of Band 2. A score of 2 balances these elements.

strengths: The report follows a logical order of sections (engagement â background â method â analysis).
Graphs are generally legible and section headings are present.
weaknesses: Frequent formatting errors (broken LaTeX, missing axis labels, legends) hinder readability.
Incomplete sentences, coding artefacts, and digressive narrative obscure focus.
Inconsistent use of terminology and units, with multiple spelling/grammar mistakes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The communication is hampered by organizational faults, missing data presentations, and formatting inconsistencies. All strands align with Band 1 descriptors (unclear or poor presentation, inconsistent terminology), justifying a score of 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: There is no mention of instrument precision, website latency, timing resolution or any quantitative uncertainty.  Measurement uncertainty is completely ignored.
Two literature references are cited in background, but the discussion does not explicitly compare findings to those studiesâ numerical outcomes or mechanisms.  The comparison remains superficial.

A logical order (Engagement â Background â Method â Analysis) exists, but essential components (raw data, statistical analysis) are missing and coding artefacts split sections.  Organisation is therefore poor.

The student proposes increasing sample size and adding repeated-interval testing and control of fatigueârealistic and relevant suggestions, though not very detailed or prioritised.  This meets the âsome realistic and relevantâ descriptor (lower end of 3-4 band).

The question (âeffect of meditation on reaction time of boys and girls aged 16-18â) is relevant and identifies key variables, but it combines two factors (meditation and sex) with no indication of how interaction will be analysed and gives no operational definition of âeffectâ.  It is therefore relevant yet not fully focused.

Limitations mention small sample and individual differences, but there is no methodological depth (e.g., order effects, learning, screen refresh rate). Discussion shows only a basic outline of practical issues.

Method merely lists using HumanBenchmark with three trials, a five-hour gap and five-minute meditation.  Sample size, sampling method, randomisation, counter-balancing and statistical plan are absent.  Most control variables are stated but not explained as to how they will be kept constant.  Thus methodology is appropriate only to a very limited extent.

Background covers a very brief definition of reaction time, mentions a couple of factors and cites two on-line sources.  No physiology of meditation, neural pathways, or statistical expectations are explored.  The information is mostly superficial and of limited relevance.
Narrative frequently digresses into restating graph observations without linking back to the question; irrelevant fragments such as personal codes appear.  Focus is weakened though not entirely lost.

Interpretation consists of descriptive statements such as âaverage decreased by 50 msâ without statistical support.  Some claims (boys and girls benefited equally) contradict earlier statements.  Interpretation is therefore insufficient and partially incorrect.

Sections are present and graphs are legible, yet numerous formatting errors (broken LaTeX, duplicate headings, missing raw tables) hinder clarity.  Understanding is possible but effortful, fitting the lower band.

The student opens with a brief personal anecdote about reaction time in sport and states that this curiosity led to the choice of meditation as an intervention.  While this shows some motivation, the narrative is short and not revisited later; there is no evidence of notable initiative beyond choosing an on-line test already designed by others.  Consequently the work demonstrates limited, not clear and sustained, engagement.

Processing is confined to calculating simple means that spreadsheet software performs automatically; no spread or inferential statistics are attempted.  Accuracy cannot be judged because raw data are hidden.  Processing is therefore basic and potentially inaccurate.

The justification links the topic to the studentâs sporting experience and claims personal curiosity, but it remains generic (âreaction time is crucial in many sportsâ).  No explicit explanation is given as to why the precise age band or meditation duration is personally significant.  Hence the justification shows only limited personal significance.

Only averaged bar charts are provided; the underlying raw times for each of the 30 individual tests (10Ã3 trials) are not shown in tables.  Quantity is therefore inadequate for independent verification.

Apart from deciding to use a free website and a five-minute YouTube video, there is little sign of the student independently shaping methodology, data processing or presentation. All graphs are simple spreadsheet outputs and there is no bespoke data table.  Evidence of personal initiative is therefore slight.
A PAR-Q is mentioned, but no consent, confidentiality or screen-time-fatigue considerations are discussed.  Safety/ethical awareness is therefore only limited.

Basic SEHS terms (reaction time, stimulus) are used correctly, but many spelling/grammar errors and inconsistent symbols (ms, \text{ ms}) are present.  Citations and units are often incorrect.  Terminology use is therefore inaccurate in several places.
A conclusion is stated (âmeditation improves reaction time slightlyâ) but rests solely on visual inspection of means and lacks quantitative support, so relevance is partial and support weak.

annotations.comment: The research question identifies key variables but combines meditation and gender effects without clarifying how interactions will be analysed or operationalised. Consider refining to focus on a single primary effect or specify how interactions will be tested.

The opening anecdote demonstrates genuine curiosity rooted in sports experience, providing initial proof of personal engagement. Maintaining this narrative thread throughout would strengthen the investigationâs personal dimension.

The background definition of reaction time is accurate but remains superficial. Expand on physiological mechanisms or neural pathways to provide a deeper context for how meditation might influence cognitive processing.

The link between meditation and reaction time is asserted but not substantiated with physiological or psychological evidence. Provide studies on attention modulation or stress reduction mechanisms to support this claim.
Mention of sex differences is brief and ends abruptly. Clarify the theoretical basis or literature rationale for expecting gender-specific effects of meditation on reaction time.

The addition to background on neurological or muscular differences is too descriptive and not critically linked to the research question. Focus on evidence directly relevant to meditationâs effects.
The primary hypothesis is clear but vague: what magnitude of decrease in reaction time would be considered significant? Include an operational definition of âdecreaseâ to guide analysis.
The methodology section lists steps but lacks justification for sample size, randomisation, and statistical plan. Explain how participants were recruited and why three trials are sufficient.
Key procedural details (group splitting, use of HumanBenchmark, breaks) are listed but donât address counterbalancing or order effects. Consider randomising meditation/noâmeditation order.
The decision to meditate for exactly five minutes is arbitrary; provide a rationale or pilot data supporting this duration as sufficient to influence reaction time.
Citing two online sources for materials is good, but the materials list itself is empty. Specify equipment needed (e.g., computer model, internet bandwidth) to assess measurement consistency.
Listing URLs is helpful, but evaluate each sourceâs credibility. Add peerâreviewed studies or explain why YouTube and HumanBenchmark are acceptable data collection tools.
The variable table clearly distinguishes independent, dependent, and controlled variables. This structured approach will aid in isolating meditation effects.
Variables are listed but controlling procedures are undeveloped. Explain how time of day or environment will be kept constant beyond stating they âstay the same.â
Safety mention via PAR-Q is a start, but lacks discussion on consent, data privacy or screen fatigue. Address ethical protocols for working with minors.
The first chart lacks clear axis labels and unitsâreaders cannot verify scale. Add ms units on both axes and label group categories explicitly.
Interpretation describes averages but omits any statistical support or uncertainty. Incorporate at least standard deviations or confidence intervals to validate claims.
The narrative in the last paragraph is cut off mid-sentence. Ensure complete sentences to maintain clarity and professional presentation.
Including error bars in Chart 1 would visually communicate variability between trials and participants, enhancing the credibility of your interpretation.
The boys-only graph lacks a legend or clear caption. Add identifying labels for colours and symbols to avoid ambiguity.
Attributing improvements solely to meditation overlooks possible learning or practice effects across trials. Discuss alternative explanations or include a no-practice control.
The meditationâcondition graph also needs axis units and a legend. Consistent presentation across charts will improve overall readability.
The small inset figure lacks context and scales, making it difficult to interpret. Provide fullâsize context or embed it in the main chart.
The conclusion as presented omits mention of random error and measurement uncertainty. Integrate a discussion of possible timing latency or software precision limitations.
Chart 5 effectively separates gender groups, allowing comparison. This visual distinction aids interpretation of genderâspecific patterns.
Interpretation of girlsâ stability after meditation is descriptive but lacks quantitative support. Include variability metrics or comparative percentages to strengthen claims.
Chart 6 shows girl trials without meditation but omits explicit axis labels. Labels for ms ranges and trial numbers are necessary for reader comprehension.
Inline LaTeX for units is inconsistent (<math>ms</math> vs âmsâ). Standardise formatting for mathematical expressions and units throughout the document.
The âEvaluation of Resultsâ header clearly signals a shift to critical reflection. Continue using explicit headings to delineate analysis, evaluation, and conclusion sections.
The evaluation restates results without assessing methodological limitations (e.g., latency, practice effects). Incorporate a deeper critique of design shortcomings.
The conclusion suggests practical applications for athletes; this is a strong link to realâworld context. However, prioritise which recommendations are most critical for future research.
Limitation discussion mentions sample size but does not quantify its impact on statistical power. Consider calculating effect sizes or power to justify sample adequacy.
Bibliography includes diverse sources but lacks uniform citation style. Align formatting (e.g., italics, punctuation) to a single style guide (APA, MLA, etc.).
The first bibliography entry runs into a URL, impairing readability. Break lines or abbreviate DOI links to maintain a clean reference section.
YouTube and online tools can be valid but require lasting URLs and access dates. Add retrieval dates or archive links to ensure reproducibility.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: Justification shows little personal significance or curiosity
Little evidence of personal input in design, implementation or presentation
Limited evidence of personal engagement with little independent thinking
Clear evidence of personal input and initiative throughout the investigation
Clear evidence of personal engagement with significant independent thinking
Justification demonstrates clear personal significance and curiosity

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Evidence of personal engagement and independent thinking

Research question stated but not focused
Methodology appropriate to very limited extent
Mainly appropriate and relevant background information
Relevant but not fully focused research question
Entirely appropriate and relevant background information
Highly appropriate methodology addressing all significant factors
Relevant and fully focused research question
Sufficient relevant raw data supporting detailed conclusion
Basic outline of practical issues only
Some relevant comparison to scientific context
Detailed, relevant and fully supported conclusion
Clear understanding of methodological issues demonstrated
Correct and justified comparison to scientific context
Many errors in terminology and conventions
Appropriate use of terminology with minimal errors
Well structured with all necessary information
